title: Fallout: Atomic Bonds Cooperative Upgrade - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0_I4PHJ-miw

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to Fallout with the Atomic Bonds Cooperative Upgrade, and his verdict is emphatic: this small box of cards turns a flawed game he liked into a great one — the way it should have shipped. These are preliminary thoughts after one play, but he's sold. He notes Fantasy Flight's habit of later "fixing" their games (Runebound's co-op, Descent's Road to Legends app); here the fix is the most transformative he's seen.

The agenda deck is gone, replaced by activation cards that drive enemies and a faction track ticking toward defeat — a stricter clock, but the necessary trade. The real magic is the new goal deck: each survivor must complete a defined objective plus a main story quest, which instantly converts Fallout into a directed open-world survival game with meaningful choices every turn. Workshop upgrades, equipment modifications, secret mutation cards that flip at five rads, an assist die for shared spaces, and fast-travel camp tokens all deepen the engine without piling on rules. Crucially it adds no library cards — and doesn't need to, since the quest deck is still enormous. A cheap, near-essential expansion he calls one of his biggest surprises.

----

Atomic Bonds is, for Daniel, the rare expansion that fixes a game's core rather than just adding content. What's an expansion that flipped your opinion of a base game from "flawed" to "keeper"?
